channel.title: Comments for page "Costume Change"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Costume Change"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And here I was trying to be distinct from Trick or Treat…but hey, it's a Lombardi tale, that's a high bar to measure up to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh…

Reminds me a bit of Mann's take on "SCP comes out to play on Halloween", but Mann's was better.

On the other hand, I do like the Mr. Shapey thing (even though that is an awkward name srsly).

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the first-person tone, which is difficult to pull off if the "author" slips in from time to time. His thoughts seemed simple and believable, and I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always liked the idea that Halloween allows abnormal things to enjoy life with the rest of us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's these inner thought stories that always get me.
Especially this one. It's innocent and creepy without really trying to be either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably. I wrote it in the course of an hour or so after coming up with the idea overnight.

Either way, I'll edit the opening paragraph to make it clear that he's not shifting shapes in open view of people. I don't know if that'll be enough to sway your vote, but I'll keep the logic potholes you pointed out in mind for later in case I decide to SCPify Shapey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting headcanon, but I think it still leaves some logical flaws lying in the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was considering writing up Mr. Shapey as an SCP to add to the series if this tale goes over well, but it doesn't make sense since he's so good at hiding and escaping from most things.

If you want the headcanon I was using when I wrote it, though, Shapey's problem is that his natural state is 'unshaped', a blobby mass of something-or-other. He can force himself to hold a stable form, but it hurts, and hurts worse the longer he maintains it, no more than a few minutes usually. Losing his hold on a shape where people can see him causes problems, and police like to shoot at horrific blob-monsters, he's got a bit of a phobia about them. So he spends most of his time hiding from the world, but gets to mingle with real people on nights like Halloween - it's easier to hold a shape when he's copying one rather than creating it from scratch then holding it.

The implication that he was shifting in view of people isn't intentional, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if I like this piece or not. I like the idea of someone who enjoys Halloween a lot because it's a day when they can turn into whatever they want, but the way you word it makes it seem like he's changing right in front of all these people. Along with that, I seem to be failing to understand why he doesn't just turn into other people during the rest of the year, or even just turn into a candy factory worker if he really likes candy that much. Also: the police officer bit didn't make much sense. Why do the police not like him?

Anyways, the premise was interesting, but I didn't like the execution as a whole. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat. Really hits the tone of the misters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ding ding ding! you win a prize, Mr. Shapey it is.

Oh, and whoever slapped the tags on before I got around to it, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Referring back to the 909 document, it appears to be a "Mr. Shapey."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. I wonder which Little Mister this one is.
